scenario: A modeler wants to indicate that a process reads from a customer database AND uses an invoice document. Team wonders if Data Object and Data Store are different concepts.
question: Data Object vs Data Store in BPMN?

options.text: Data Object and Data Store represent the same concept in BPMN â€” this approach has limitations. They model fundamentally different data constructs. A Data Object (page with folded corner icon) represents in-flight data within a process instance, such as a document or artifact being processed. A Data Store (cylinder icon) represents external persistent storage outside the process, such as a database or repository that persists independently of any process instance. Using Data Objects for persistent storage or Data Stores for process-scoped data misrepresents the data architecture of the system.

Different concepts: Data Object (document icon -- page with folded corner) represents data flowing within a process instance, typically a business document or artifact being processed (invoice, customer record, application form). Data Store (cylinder icon) represents persistent external data -- a database, repository, or external storage system that the process reads from or writes to, independent of the process lifecycle. In Camunda 8, both are primarily documentary elements used for visual clarity in diagrams rather than enforced runtime constructs; actual data handling uses process variables, Document Handling API, and worker-accessed external systems.

Neither Data Objects nor Data Stores are executed or enforced as runtime variables in Camunda 8. They are documentary BPMN elements used for visual communication of data flows in diagrams. Actual process data is managed through process variables (set via Input/Output Mappings, worker completions), the Document Handling API (for binary content), and external system access via Connectors or workers. Treating Data Objects as variable definitions will cause confusion about how Camunda 8 actually manages process state.

Data Store has been removed from BPMN modeling -- this claim has no basis in either the BPMN specification or Camunda 8 documentation. Data Store remains a valid BPMN element available in Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler for diagramming purposes. It visually represents persistent external storage that a process reads from or writes to (databases, repositories). While Camunda 8 treats Data Store as documentary rather than executable, the element continues to be available in the modeler palette and is used in complex process diagrams to communicate data architecture to stakeholders and analysts.

a.text: Incorrect. BPMN Data semantics: Data Object (BPMN spec): Notation: document icon (page with folded corner). Represents: data within the process -- flowing between activities. Lifecycle: tied to process instance. Example: Invoice document, customer record, application form being processed. Data Store (BPMN spec): Notation: cylinder icon (like a database symbol). Represents: persistent data outside the process -- accessed by activities. Lifecycle: independent of process; lives in external system. Example: Customer database, document repository, knowledge base. Difference: Data Object: data IS the process artefact. Data Store: data is in EXTERNAL store. Visual associations: Data Association (dashed line): connects activity to data object/store. Direction: activity reads from / writes to data. In Camunda 8 (runtime impact): Reality check: Data Objects + Data Stores are typically documentary in Camunda 8. Not enforced at runtime: don't constrain what variables exist or how external systems are accessed. Process variables do the actual data carrying within process. Document Handling does the actual document management. External APIs (via Connectors / workers) access databases. Use for:
